questions.text: I find that writing everything down makes me feel constrained rather than empowered.
I make lists for grocery shopping, packing, and other routine tasks.
I often wait for the group to signal readiness before pursuing change.
I hold the view that calm, data-informed analysis of painful situations is the most reliable foundation for decisions that have serious consequences for others.
I hold the view that the obligation to take suffering seriously is one of the few moral principles I would never negotiate.
When I'm excited about something, I feel it in every cell of my body.
I sense when someone is restraining themselves from saying what they really think.
I experience loyalty and attachment with a fierceness that can be difficult to regulate.
I tend not to notice when someone softens a criticism to spare feelings.
I hold the view that my even-keeled nature is a core part of my personality, not a lack of feeling.
I consistently set long-term goals and build step-by-step plans to reach them.
I feel that the weight I assign to others' suffering is the thread that connects my daily choices to my deepest values.
I believe I have a talent for seeing what needs to be done and making it happen.
I routinely accept the worst option for myself so that others can have the better one.
I feel that it is my responsibility to point the way when people are lost.
I feel like I can sense what a group's unwritten rules are without being told.
I believe that the most important thing I can do with my life is reduce others' burdens.
I find that I naturally gravitate toward opportunities that advance my own position.
